structured_choices.data_source: Public package registry metadata (npm and/or PyPI), source code snapshots from linked repositories, dependency graphs, commit histories, and vulnerability disclosure records (NVD, GitHub Security Advisories, OSV database). Data spans 4–5 years of historical records with sufficient lookback to establish baseline vulnerability rates. Realistic dataset: 50,000–100,000 open-source packages with complete metadata coverage.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary label: 1 if a package received a confirmed CVE assignment (or was included in a major security advisory) within a 12-month forward window after the snapshot date; 0 if no confirmed CVE was discovered in that window and the package was not censored (i.e., still actively maintained or had sufficient observation time). Labeling uses CVE records published in NVD or OSV, with a 30-day grace period after snapshot to allow for initial disclosure lag. Packages with <3 months of observation in the forward window are excluded to reduce censoring bias.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal (chronological) split with no data leakage. All data is snapshots taken at discrete time points. Training set: packages with snapshots in months 1–24. Validation set: packages with snapshots in months 25–30, labeled using CVEs discovered in months 25–42. Test set: packages with snapshots in months 31–36, labeled using CVEs discovered in months 31–48. This ensures the model never sees future CVE information during training or validation. The temporal structure reflects the realistic deployment scenario in which the model scores new or updated packages and must predict future risk.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing, feature scaling, vocabulary building (for code tokenization), and embedding models are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–24). Fitted transformers and tokenizers are applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents leakage of validation or test statistics into training. Dependency graph encodings and commit history aggregations are computed independently for each package at its snapshot date; no cross-package normalization or statistics are computed on the validation or test sets.
structured_choices.features: Three feature groups: (1) Source code features: abstract syntax tree (AST) depth statistics, token frequency distributions, cyclomatic complexity, lines of code, and transformer-based code embeddings (e.g., from CodeBERT, fit on training code only); (2) Dependency metadata: dependency count, depth of dependency tree, fraction of pinned vs. loose version constraints, age of dependencies, transitive dependency count, and graph centrality measures; (3) Historical patterns: commit frequency (per month in past 12 months), time since last commit, number of committers, commit message keywords related to bug fixes or security patches, release frequency, and fraction of commits touching version or security-related files. All time-based features are computed relative to the snapshot date. Features are standardized (z-score) using training set statistics; categorical features are one-hot encoded using training set vocabulary only.
structured_choices.model: Transformer-based model (e.g., fine-tuned CodeBERT or DistilBERT) that ingests: (a) tokenized source code; (b) embedded dependency metadata (concatenated or cross-attended with code embeddings); (c) temporal feature vectors (commit and release patterns). The architecture concatenates or cross-attends these three inputs before a final classification head. Hyperparameters (learning rate, attention heads, dropout, fine-tuning epochs) are tuned on the validation set only, not on test.
structured_choices.baseline: A logistic regression model trained on the same feature set (source code + dependency metadata + historical patterns), with identical preprocessing (fit on training, applied to val/test). This baseline receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort: grid search over regularization (L2 penalty) and class weight balancing on the validation set. The source-code-only variant (as cited in the hypothesis) is a secondary baseline: logistic regression using only AST and code embedding features, also tuned on validation. Both baselines use the identical train/val/test splits and labeling scheme, ensuring fair comparison. The transformer model is considered the primary intervention.
structured_choices.primary_metric: PR-AUC (area under the precision-recall curve), because the task has severe class imbalance (CVEs are rare; typical base rates 2–5%) and the operational objective is to flag high-risk packages under a constrained alert budget. PR-AUC is insensitive to the true negative rate and directly reflects precision and recall tradeoffs relevant to security operations. Reported at the test set only.
secondary_metrics: Precision at fixed recall levels (e.g., precision when recalling 80% of true positives), to operationalize 'alert budget' constraints.
ROC-AUC, to detect whether the model is better than random across all thresholds (a sanity check).
Calibration: expected calibration error (ECE) or Brier score, to ensure predicted probabilities are well-calibrated (important for practitioners to trust risk scores).
False positive rate at a fixed threshold (e.g., the threshold that achieves 80% recall), to estimate alert fatigue impact.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (months 25–30, labeled with months 25–42 CVEs). Hyperparameter tuning is performed by grid search or Bayesian optimization on the validation PR-AUC only. The test set is not accessed during tuning. A small held-out development set (5–10% of training) may be used for early stopping, but only on the training set.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 31–36 with forward labels in months 31–48) is locked and evaluated exactly once, after all model selection and hyperparameter tuning is complete. Final metrics (primary PR-AUC and all secondaries) are reported on the test set only. No threshold adjustment, re-weighting, or post-hoc modifications are applied based on test performance.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Three confound checks are performed on the test set to ensure the model is not simply learning package popularity or maintainer activity: (1) Stratified analysis: test-set performance is reported separately for packages grouped by commit frequency (low, medium, high) and by dependency count (small, large). If PR-AUC is substantially higher in one stratum, it suggests the model is exploiting activity proxies rather than true vulnerability patterns. (2) Ablation: the transformer model is compared against a variant that uses only dependency and temporal features (no source code), to verify that source code contributes meaningfully and the transformer is not simply learning from metadata. (3) Residual confound correlation: for the test set, Spearman correlation is computed between model predictions and package popularity proxies (total downloads, number of dependents) and reported as a sensitivity measure. If correlation is very high (>0.7), the model may be conflating popularity with risk.

design_narrative:
To test whether a transformer-based model incorporating source code, dependency metadata, and historical vulnerability-fix patterns outperforms a model using source code alone, we propose the following methodologically sound experiment:

**1. Data Collection and Labeling**

We will use a historical dataset spanning 4–5 years of public package registries (npm and/or PyPI). We target 50,000–100,000 packages with complete metadata: source code snapshots, dependency graphs, commit histories, and linked vulnerability records from NVD and GitHub Security Advisories. Labels are derived from CVE assignments: a package is labeled positive if it received a confirmed CVE within 12 months of the snapshot date; it is labeled negative if no CVE was discovered in that window and the package was not censored (i.e., had sufficient observation time or remained actively maintained). This definition reflects the realistic deployment scenario in which a model must predict future vulnerability disclosures.

**2. Temporal Split to Prevent Leakage**

Critically, we use a chronological split rather than random sampling. All package snapshots are dated; training data comprises snapshots from months 1–24, validation snapshots from months 25–30 (with forward labels from months 25–42), and test snapshots from months 31–36 (with forward labels from months 31–48). This temporal structure ensures the model never sees future CVE information during training or validation, reflecting the real-world scenario in which the model scores packages and must predict risks that will be discovered later. The 6-month gap between validation and test windows provides a buffer to further reduce potential bleed-through of disclosure patterns.

**3. Feature Engineering**

We construct three integrated feature groups: (a) source code features including AST statistics, token distributions, complexity metrics, and transformer-based embeddings (CodeBERT, fit on training code only); (b) dependency metadata (count, tree depth, version constraint patterns, transitive dependencies, and graph measures); and (c) historical patterns (commit frequency, recency, contributor count, commit message signals, and release velocity). All preprocessing (standardization, tokenization, embedding) is fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test. This prevents information leakage.

**4. Model and Baselines**

The primary model is a transformer architecture that cross-attends source code embeddings, dependency metadata embeddings, and temporal feature vectors before a final classification head. We compare this against two fair baselines: (i) logistic regression on the full feature set (source code + metadata + history), receiving equal hyperparameter tuning effort; and (ii) logistic regression on source code features only. Both baselines use identical preprocessing, splits, and evaluation, ensuring a level comparison.

**5. Evaluation Metrics**

The primary metric is PR-AUC, selected because the task exhibits severe class imbalance (CVEs are rare: 2–5% base rate) and the operational objective is high precision under a constrained alert budget. PR-AUC is directly sensitive to the precision-recall tradeoff critical for security operations. Secondary metrics include precision at fixed recall levels (e.g., 80% recall), ROC-AUC, calibration error, and false positive rate, to provide operational context and sanity checks.

**6. Model Selection and Test Protocol**

Hyperparameter tuning (learning rate, regularization, class weighting) is performed by grid search on the validation set PR-AUC only. The test set is locked and evaluated exactly once after all tuning is complete. No threshold adjustment or re-weighting is applied post-hoc based on test results.

**7. Confound Controls**

To address the risk that the model learns package popularity or maintainer activity rather than true vulnerability risk, we perform three checks on the test set: (i) stratified analysis reporting PR-AUC separately by commit frequency and dependency count; (ii) ablation comparing the full transformer against a metadata-and-history-only variant; and (iii) correlation of model predictions with popularity proxies (downloads, dependents). These checks will reveal whether the lift is driven by vulnerability patterns or confounding factors.

**Claims and Scope**

This design will establish whether the transformer model with integrated features achieves higher PR-AUC than the baseline models on held-out temporal test data, under realistic label noise and censoring. It will not establish causation (correlation with a latent vulnerability-generation process), and results may be limited by label incompleteness (many vulnerabilities are never disclosed). The design is robust to temporal leakage and ensures fair comparison across models.
